title: Permanent Marker on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes; fiber check before any solvent
stainChemistry: Permanent marker (Sharpie-class) is concentrated solvent-based dye suspended in xylene / toluene / alcohol carriers, plus a resin binder. When the solvent evaporates, the resin locks the dye into the fiber mechanically and chemically — that's the "permanent" part. Black Sharpie is the most concentrated pigment; colors are slightly easier. On carpet, IPA (91% isopropyl alcohol) is the safest effective solvent — dissolves the resin and lifts the dye onto a catch cloth. The risky step is SOLVENT on UNKNOWN FIBER: acetone destroys acetate/modacrylic/triacetate fibers, and strong solvents can distort olefin/polypropylene. The #1 internet bad advice here is pouring alcohol or acetone directly on the spot — floods the pad with dye AND solvent, spreading the stain and risking fiber damage. Catch cloth UNDER + tamp solvent from TOP is the discipline.
whyThisWorks: IPA softens the resin and lifts the dye. A catch cloth UNDER the carpet pile captures the dissolved dye as it moves through the fiber. Tamping from the TOP, outside-in, keeps the stain from spreading. Dawn clears residual solvent. Fiber check FIRST prevents the "I used acetone and the carpet melted" failure mode. Over-wetting is the other failure mode — pad soak with solvent-laden dye is functionally permanent.
defaultAssumption: Kid with a Sharpie on the living room rug. Fresh = real chance. Dried in = harder but still solvent-responsive with multiple rounds. Black = hardest; colors = easier. Carpet fiber is usually nylon or polyester — IPA-tolerant. Olefin (Berber, indoor-outdoor) is solvent-sensitive. Wool and acetate = pro.

spottingProtocol.technique: Verify solvent safe before touching the stain
spottingProtocol.instruction: In a hidden area (closet, under furniture): dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a white cloth, press on hidden carpet 30 sec, lift. Cloth picks up fiber / color, or carpet texture changes = solvent NOT safe. STOP, call a pro. Clean test = nylon / polyester, proceed.

spottingProtocol.technique: Dry blot to absorb wet ink before solvent
spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cotton towel, paper towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: If wet: press paper towels STRAIGHT DOWN, lift, press fresh ones. No wiping. If possible, slide a white cotton catch cloth UNDER the carpet pile (rug edge, or through a hole) so dissolved ink transfers DOWN. For wall-to-wall carpet, work the catch cloth as deep into the pile as possible.

Apply IPA to a white cloth (never pour on carpet). Tamp from the top, outside-in. Flip cloth as it picks up ink. 3–5 passes typical. Over-wetting = pad soak = permanent. Ventilate — flammable. No flames.

spottingProtocol.agent: If IPA Plateaus — Graduate With Caution
spottingProtocol.technique: Amyl acetate or acetone ONLY if fiber-tested
spottingProtocol.equipment: Amyl acetate (pro) OR acetone (tested fibers only)
spottingProtocol.instruction: If IPA stops progressing and hidden-seam test was fully clean, stronger solvent may help. Re-test acetone at the hidden seam. Still clean = tamp carefully from top. Any doubt = stop, go pro. NEVER acetone on untested fiber.

spottingProtocol.technique: Clear solvent residue + lift remaining pigment
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dawn + cool water on damp cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dawn in cool water on damp cloth. Work with soft toothbrush. Damp cloth rinse, never pour water.
Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan-dry. Full solvent clearance takes hours. Ghost remaining = one more tamp pass OR escalate.

steps: 1. Fiber check at hidden seam with 91% IPA.
3. IPA tamp from top, outside-in, multiple passes.
4. Graduate to amyl acetate / acetone ONLY with fiber confirmation.
warnings: FIBER CHECK FIRST — acetone destroys acetate/modacrylic/triacetate
NEVER pour solvent on carpet — tamp with cloth
Flammable solvent — ventilate, no flame, no smoking
Never rub — drags ink through fiber

homeSolutions.agent: Fiber Check FIRST — Before Any Solvent
homeSolutions.instruction: Before any chemistry touches the spot, check what your carpet is made of. Most home carpet is nylon or polyester and tolerates rubbing alcohol. But ACETONE destroys acetate, modacrylic, and triacetate fibers on contact, and can soften olefin (polypropylene, which is what a lot of Berbers and indoor-outdoor carpets are). Wool carpet is pro-only. Hidden-seam test: in a closet or under furniture, dab 91% isopropyl alcohol on a white rag, press on the hidden spot for 30 seconds. If the rag picks up carpet fiber or color, or the carpet feels different after, STOP. Call a pro. Clean test = proceed.

homeSolutions.agent: Catch Cloth UNDER the Pile + Blot
homeSolutions.instruction: If the ink is still wet (within minutes of drawing on carpet): press paper towels STRAIGHT DOWN on the spot — don't wipe. Lift, press fresh paper towels, repeat. For area rugs, slip a clean white cotton cloth UNDERNEATH the pile — dissolved ink transfers DOWN into it rather than spreading sideways. On wall-to-wall carpet, work a catch cloth as deep into the pile as you can get it.

homeSolutions.agent: 91% Rubbing Alcohol on a White Rag — Never Poured
homeSolutions.instruction: Put 91% isopropyl rubbing alcohol (drugstore, higher % than the standard 70%) on a clean white rag. DO NOT pour it on the carpet — pouring sinks ink and solvent into the pad where you can't extract it, and that's the failure mode. Press and tamp the rag on the spot from the TOP. Work from the OUTSIDE of the stain toward the CENTER (counter-intuitive but prevents spreading). Flip the rag frequently as it picks up ink. 3–5 passes typical for fresh Sharpie. Open windows — rubbing alcohol is flammable. No candles, no gas stove running.

If IPA runs out of effect and your fiber test was CLEAN and CONFIRMED nylon or polyester, regular acetone (nail polish remover, acetone-type) is stronger. Re-test acetone at the hidden seam first. Still clean = same technique: rag, tamp, outside-in, cold. If there was ANY doubt on the first fiber test, don't graduate to acetone — call a pro. Acetone on acetate/modacrylic/triacetate fiber DESTROYS it.
Work a drop of Dawn with cool water into the spot using a soft toothbrush. Clears the solvent residue and lifts any last pigment. Damp cloth rinse — don't pour water.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, 30 minutes. Fan the area. Full solvent clearance takes several hours. Don't walk on the spot until dry. Inspect in daylight after drying. Ghost ring remaining = one more IPA tamp round OR escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Olefin, Wool, or Stubborn Ink
homeSolutions.instruction: Pro if: the carpet is wool, Berber, or indoor-outdoor (often olefin), if the hidden-seam test showed fiber interaction, if the ink is deeply set into the pile, or if IPA + Dawn didn't clear it. Tell the pro: "Permanent marker on [nylon / polyester / olefin / unknown] carpet, age [fresh / dried], home-treated with 91% IPA tamped from top + Dawn. No pouring. No heat. [Fiber test was clean / showed color pickup / unknown fiber]. [Residual ghost / fiber damage visible]."

steps: 1. FIBER CHECK at hidden seam with 91% IPA BEFORE anything.
2. Catch cloth UNDER pile if possible. Blot wet ink straight down.
3. 91% IPA on a RAG, tamp from top, outside-in. NEVER pour.
4. Graduate to acetone ONLY if fiber-confirmed clean — otherwise stop.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the hidden-seam test damages carpet fiber, if the carpet is wool / Berber / indoor-outdoor (often olefin), if IPA can't progress and you aren't fiber-confirmed for acetone, or if the ink is deeply set.
warnings: FIBER CHECK FIRST — acetone destroys several common carpet fibers
NEVER pour solvent on carpet — always tamp with cloth
Never wipe or rub — drags ink through fiber

neverDo: Never wipe ink — blot straight down or tamp
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester carpet: tolerates IPA, usually acetone (test)
Olefin (polypropylene): IPA OK, acetone can soften — test carefully
Acetate / modacrylic / triacetate: acetone DESTROYS — pro only
Berber / indoor-outdoor: often olefin — test carefully

materialWarnings: Solvent on unknown fiber is high-risk

escalation.when: Hidden-seam fiber test shows interaction, carpet is wool / Berber / indoor-outdoor (olefin), IPA + Dawn plateau with ink remaining, or acetone can't be confirmed fiber-safe.
escalation.whatToTell: "Permanent marker on [nylon / polyester / olefin / unknown] carpet, age [fresh / dried], home-treated with 91% IPA tamped from top + Dawn + no pouring + no heat. [Fiber test clean / fiber test showed color pickup / fiber unknown]. [Residual ghost / solvent sensitivity]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with solvent spotting + fiber ID training

contextQuestions: Carpet fiber — nylon / polyester / olefin / wool / unknown?
Sharpie black or color? (black is most pigmented)
Has anyone already poured alcohol / acetone on it?
How deep into the pile did ink go?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Fiber identity is 90% of the battle. Black Sharpie is the hardest variant — expect multiple rounds even on fresh. Previous poured-solvent attempts often create a permanent ghost because ink + solvent reached pad depth.
